summary:
The Austronesian maritime expansion represents one of the most remarkable episodes of human migration in prehistory — the spread of Austronesian-speaking peoples from their ancestral homeland in Taiwan across the islands of Southeast Asia, the Pacific, and eventually the Indian Ocean, colonizing an area spanning more than half the globe's circumference. The earliest phase of this expansion, originating in [[event:1610|Neolithic Taiwan]] communities around 4000–3500 BCE, set in motion a series of migrations that would eventually place Austronesian languages and cultural traditions from Madagascar to Easter Island.

The ancestral Austronesian homeland is generally identified with Neolithic Taiwan, where agricultural communities practicing rice farming, pig and dog domestication, and a distinctive red-slipped pottery tradition developed the seafaring skills and cultural impetus for maritime expansion. The initial movement from Taiwan southward into the Philippines, dated to approximately 3500–3000 BCE, was followed by progressive expansion through Borneo and the Indonesian archipelago and into the Pacific. By around 1500–1000 BCE, Austronesian-speaking Lapita culture colonists had reached the remote Pacific islands of Fiji, Tonga, and Samoa; subsequent migrations eventually reached Polynesia, with Hawaii settled around 400 CE, New Zealand around 1200 CE, and Easter Island around 1000 CE.

The technological basis of Austronesian expansion was the outrigger canoe — a double-outrigger or single-outrigger vessel providing stability in ocean swells — combined with sophisticated navigational knowledge using stars, ocean swells, wind patterns, bird behavior, and cloud formations. These technologies enabled open-ocean voyages across hundreds and sometimes thousands of kilometers of open water, a feat of seamanship unmatched anywhere in the world at comparable periods. The Austronesians were not merely coastal voyagers but systematic explorers and colonists of the open ocean.

Austronesian expansion also carried a distinctive cultural and agricultural package. Austronesian colonists brought their characteristic crops — taro, yams, breadfruit, bananas, and coconuts — as well as domesticated pigs, chickens, and dogs to each new island environment. This deliberate transplantation of agricultural systems into virgin island ecosystems allowed colonists to establish productive food systems in previously uninhabited environments. The Austronesian spread represents the last major episode of human colonization of previously uninhabited landmasses before the modern era.

The scale and success of Austronesian maritime expansion places it among the great episodes of human prehistory. It demonstrates the extraordinary navigational capabilities of prehistoric Pacific peoples and stands as a reminder that complex, organized long-distance voyaging was not a feature exclusively of literate or metal-using societies. The Austronesian world, stretching from Madagascar to Easter Island, remains one of the largest culturally connected regions in human history.
